---
title: What's the Weight?
subtitle: |
  Estimating Controlled Outcome Differences in \
  Complex Surveys for Health Disparities Research
author: 
  name: Stephen Salerno, PhD
  affiliation: Washington University in St. Louis
date: 2026-07-17
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
bibliography: ref.bib
csl: nature.csl

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
    logo: images/theme/logo.png
    footer: "Joint HPSS and SRMS Webinar"
    slide-number: true
    hash: true
    navigation-mode: linear
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: true
    center: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    preview-links: true
    plugins: [search, zoom, chalkboard]
    code-line-numbers: true
    embed-resources: true
    
resources:
  - images/
    
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: "images/theme/bg1.png"
  data-background-size: "auto 100%"
  data-background-position: "right"

execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## First, Some Acknowledgements {.theme-bg3}

### Funded by the Sloan Precision Oncology Technology Dissemination Award

::: {.people-row}

::: {.person}

![](images/people/awan.jpg){.headshot}

**Awan Afiaz, MS**  
*PhD Student*  
Public Health Sciences, Fred Hutch  
Biostatistics, University of Washington

:::

::: {.person}

![](images/people/barbara.jpg){.headshot}

**Barbara Lam, MD**  
*Assistant Professor*  
Clinical Research Division, Fred Hutch  
Hematology-Oncology, UW Medicine  

:::

::: {.person}

![](images/people/jeff.jpeg){.headshot}

**Jeff Leek, PhD**    
*Vice President and Chief Data Officer*  
*Professor, Biostatistics Program*  
*J. Orin Edson Foundation Endowed Chair*  
Public Health Sciences, Fred Hutch  
Biostatistics, University of Washington 

:::

:::

## Our Motivation {.theme-bg3}

### Telomere Length and its Relationship with Race and SES

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}

* Regions of DNA at the ends of chromosomes that protect against cell death
* Shortening associated w/ cardiometabolic outcomes 
* Affected by age, sex, race/ethnicity, genetics, 
* SES, environment, psychosocial stress, ...
* Longer telomeres in Black individuals (paradox)

::: {.fragment}
*BUT*
:::

* Comparable length in populations w/ similar SES

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

<br>

![[Image Generated using GPT 5.2]{.caption}](images/telomeres.png){text-align="center"}

:::
:::

# "If we could hypothetically balance SES between Black and White individuals in a nationally representative sample, would we still see significant Black/White differences in telomere length?" {.theme-bg2}

::: footer
:::

## Our Data {.theme-bg3}

### National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

* Nationally representative survey by the CDC
* Rich data from interviews, physical 
* examinations, laboratory tests, …
* Stratified, clustered complex design:
* Primary sampling units (counties) 
* Drawn from demographic-specific strata
* Oversamples non-Hispanic Black participants and ≤ 130% poverty limit

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

![[Image Generated using GPT 5.2]{.caption}](images/nhanes-sampling.png)

:::
:::

## What inspired *scorcher's* design {.theme-bg3}

### And where/why we deviate

<br>

::: {layout="[[1,1],[1,1],[1,1],[1,1]]"}

**Inspired by:**

**We are creating:**

::: {.fragment fragment-index="1"}

<br>

The [Keras](https://keras.io/) Functional API in Python[@gulli2017deep]

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index="2"}

<br>

An API for functional model graphs, multi-input/output models, nonlinear topologies

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index="3"}

<br>

The [keras3](https://keras3.posit.co/) R package[@chollet2022deep]

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index="4"}

<br>

Tidy, pipable functions that fit naturally into R-based analysis pipelines

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index="5"}

<br>

The [torch](https://torch.mlverse.org/) R package ([mlverse](https://www.mlverse.org/))[@chowdhry2025deep]

:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index="6"}

<br>

A package that is 100% native to R, without needing Python or reticulate

:::

:::

## Write models like sentences via composable functions {.theme-bg3}

*scorcher* lets you develop models by focusing on 
*what the model should do*, not *how to write code for it*

<br>

::: {.scorch-flow}

::: {.flow-pair .fragment data-fragment-index="1"}
::: {.flow-text}
Load the data
:::
::: {.flow-code}
`scorch_dataloader()`
:::
:::

::: {.flow-step .fragment data-fragment-index="2"}
::: {.flow-text}
then
:::
::: {.flow-arrow}
|>
:::
:::

::: {.flow-pair .fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
::: {.flow-text}
add layers
:::
::: {.flow-code}
`scorch_layer()`
:::
:::

::: {.flow-step .fragment data-fragment-index="4"}
::: {.flow-text}
then
:::
::: {.flow-arrow}
|>
:::
:::

::: {.flow-pair .fragment data-fragment-index="5"}
::: {.flow-text}
compile the model
:::
::: {.flow-code}
`compile_scorch()`
:::
:::

::: {.flow-step .fragment data-fragment-index="6"}
::: {.flow-text}
then
:::
::: {.flow-arrow}
|>
:::
:::

::: {.flow-pair .fragment data-fragment-index="7"}
::: {.flow-text}
fit the model
:::
::: {.flow-code}
`fit_scorch()`
:::
:::

:::

## This keeps architectures explicit and readable ... {.theme-bg3}

### MNIST CNN in 20 lines w/ *scorcher*

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1|2|3-15|16-19|20"
library(scorcher)
dl <- scorch_create_dataloader(x_train, y_train, batch_size = 500)
scorch_model <- initiate_scorch(dl) |>
  scorch_input("x") |>
  scorch_layer("conv1", conv2d, 1, 32, 3) |>
  scorch_layer("act1",  relu) |>
  scorch_layer("conv2", conv2d, 32, 64, 3) |>
  scorch_layer("act2",  relu) |>
  scorch_layer("pool1", max_pool2d, 2) |>
  scorch_dropout("drop1", p = 0.25) |>
  scorch_flatten("flat1") |>
  scorch_layer("fc1", linear, 9216, 128) |>
  scorch_layer("act3", relu) |>
  scorch_layer("fc2", linear, 128, 10) |>
  scorch_output("fc2") |>
  compile_scorch(
    loss_fn          = nn_cross_entropy_loss(),
    optimizer_fn     = optim_adam,
    optimizer_params = list(lr = 0.001))  |>
  fit_scorch(num_epochs = 10, verbose = TRUE)
```

:::

::: {.column .center width="60%"}

![](images/mnist.png){width="100%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

* **Data:** 70k grayscale handwritten digits, from 0 to 9
* **Goal:** Classify images into categories (0-9)
* **Result:** Test accuracy of 98.56% after 10 epochs

:::

:::

## ... even for complex architectures {.theme-bg3}

### Example Diffusion Model w/ the Datasaurus Dozen

<br>

* **Task:** Generate an image of a T-Rex from random noise
* **Result:** *Scorcher* produces model with 85% less code
* **Benefit:** *Scorcher* code is: 
  - Human-readable
  - Easy to explain *and* reproduce
  - No prior knowledge of Torch syntax

::: {.absolute top="5%" left="70%"}

![<span class="caption">Images and Python Code: [github.com/tanelp/tiny-diffusion](github.com/tanelp/tiny-diffusion)</span>](images/tiny-diffusion.png){width="100&" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

# A Clinical Example {.theme-bg2}

::: footer
:::

## We are piloting *scorcher* for clinical research {.theme-bg3}

### Multimodal pulmonary embolism risk prediction

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

::: {.fragment}

* 3rd most common cause of *cardiovascular death*
* Screening relies on *subjective* decision rules
* *Gold standard* CTPA is expensive, involves radiation, and is not always readily available

:::

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

![<span class="caption">Source: https://www.myphteam.com/resources/can-pulmonary-embolism-cause-ph-lung-sounds-and-more</span>](images/pe.png){width="100%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

:::

## Medical imaging provides a potential alternative  {.theme-bg3}

### Objective, standardized, available before clinical probability is formally assessed

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="70%"}

* Routinely obtained in the emergency department:
    - Chest X-Rays (CXR)
    - Electrocardiograms (EKG) 
* Prior AI/ML work has used:
    - EKG alone[@silva2023artificial;@wysokinski2024electrocardiogram]
    - EKG with EHR data[@somani2022development]
* No prior work with CXR

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

![<span class="caption">Source: https://jbsr.be/articles/10.5334/jbsr.3052</span>](images/cxr.png){width="100%" height="auto" fig-align="center" margin-bottom="-100px"}

![<span class="caption">Source: https://epomedicine.com/emergency-medicine/ecg-changes-in-pulmonary-embolism/</span>](images/ekg.png){width="100%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

:::

## Preliminary study using MIMIC-IV[@johnson2023author] {.theme-bg3}

### 886 adult patients undergoing CTPA for PE with CXR and EKG < 48 hr before CTPA

<br>

* 212 PE+ (23.9%) classified by clinical review of CTPA reports
* CXR: 1,376-dimensional embeddings from EfficientNet-L2 pre-trained on chest radiographs
* EKG: Raw 12-lead waveform data (5,000 samples/lead, 500 Hz)
* Stratified 70/15/15 (train/test/validation), prioritizing sensitivity for screening

## Fusion CNN model {.theme-bg3}

### A common model structure in biomedical applications

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

<br>

1. Modality-specific arms
2. Join (concatenate) embeddings
3. Shared subnetwork
4. Output risk score

::: {.fragment}

93.9% Sensitivity (Revised Geneva: 91%),[@lucassen2011clinical] 
22.5% Specificity, 0.48 AUPRC (2x baseline)

:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![](images/fusion.png){width="75%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

:::

## Next steps {.theme-bg3}

### What we're building toward:

* A more mature functional graph-based API
* Support for chained pipelines/workflows (e.g., segmentation -> classification -> prediction)
* Infrastructure for distributed/federated learning
* Better evaluation helpers
* Better interoperability with the mlverse
* Reproducible artifacts (e.g., serialized configs/checkpoints, run logs, architecture diagrams)

## Open source collaboration is the fastest path to success {.theme-bg3}

### Train -> predict -> evaluate, all inside one reproducible R pipeline

<br>

Deep learning infrastructure improves fastest when:

* APIs are shaped by real use cases
* Features emerge from community needs
* Bugs are found early, in diverse environments

::: {.fragment} 
**If this interests you:**
:::

* Try *scorcher* in your own research
* Open issues and let us know your experience
* Help us define evaluation standards

:::

## Thank You! {.theme-bg3}

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**These Slides:** 

[salernos.github.io/2026-enar-scorcher/](https://salernos.github.io/2026-enar-scorcher/)

![](images/qr_slides.png){width="75%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Scorcher Repo:** 

[github.com/jtleek/scorcher](https://github.com/jtleek/scorcher)

![](images/qr_github.png){width="75%" height="auto" fig-align="center"}

:::

:::


## References {.theme-bg3}

<br>

:::{#refs}
:::

## No object-oriented programming required {.theme-bg3}

### Here is an example 'simple' CNN for MNIST

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "1-4|5-14|15-29|30-62"
library(torch)
library(torchvision)
train_dl <- dataloader(train_ds, batch_size = 32, shuffle = TRUE)
test_dl  <- dataloader(test_ds, batch_size = 32)
net <- nn_module(
  "Net",
  initialize = function() {
    self$conv1 <- nn_conv2d(1, 32, 3, 1)
    self$conv2 <- nn_conv2d(32, 64, 3, 1)
    self$dropout1 <- nn_dropout2d(0.25)
    self$dropout2 <- nn_dropout2d(0.5)
    self$fc1 <- nn_linear(9216, 128)
    self$fc2 <- nn_linear(128, 10)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    x <- self$conv1(x)
    x <- nnf_relu(x)
    x <- self$conv2(x)
    x <- nnf_relu(x)
    x <- nnf_max_pool2d(x, 2)
    x <- self$dropout1(x)
    x <- torch_flatten(x, start_dim = 2)
    x <- self$fc1(x)
    x <- nnf_relu(x)
    x <- self$dropout2(x)
    output <- self$fc2(x)
    output
  }
)
model <- net()
device <- if(cuda_is_available()) "cuda" else "cpu"
model$to(device = device)
optimizer <- optim_sgd(model$parameters, lr = 0.01)
epochs <- 10
for (epoch in 1:10) {
  pb <- progress::progress_bar$new(
    total = length(train_dl),
    format = "[:bar] :eta Loss: :loss"
  )
  train_losses <- c()
  test_losses <- c()
  coro::loop(for (b in train_dl) {
    optimizer$zero_grad()
    output <- model(b[[1]]$to(device = device))
    loss <- nnf_cross_entropy(output, b[[2]]$to(device = device))
    loss$backward()
    optimizer$step()
    train_losses <- c(train_losses, loss$item())
    pb$tick(tokens = list(loss = mean(train_losses)))
  })
  with_no_grad({
    coro::loop(for (b in test_dl) {
      model$eval()
      output <- model(b[[1]]$to(device = device))
      loss <- nnf_cross_entropy(output, b[[2]]$to(device = device))
      test_losses <- c(test_losses, loss$item())
      model$train()
    })
  })
  cat(sprintf("Loss at epoch %d [Train: %3f] [Test: %3f]\n",
              epoch, mean(train_losses), mean(test_losses)))
}
```

:::

::: {.column width="60%"}

<br>

This requires:

* Defing modules and mutable objects by hand
* Writing custom forward methods and training loops
* Relying on PyTorch inheritance conventions

:::

:::

## Fit separate CNNs on each modality {.theme-bg3}

<br>

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

### CXR Only

::: {.fragment}

* 3,590 embeddings, 8.4% prevalence

* AUPRC: 0.152 (1.8x baseline)

* Sensitivity: 29.0%

* Specificity: 73.0%

:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### EKG Only

::: {.fragment}

* 2,925 records, 23.5% prevalence

* AUPRC: 0.392 (1.7x baseline)

* Sensitivity: 88.5%

* Specificity: 20.9%

:::

:::

:::
